sections.title: Overview
sections.content:
Varicella (chickenpox) is caused by Varicella-Zoster Virus (VZV), a highly contagious herpesvirus. Primary infection presents as chickenpox. Reactivation of latent virus causes herpes zoster (shingles).

**Incubation:** 14–16 days (range 10–21 days)

**Infectious period:** 1–2 days before rash appears until **all lesions have crusted over** (typically 5–7 days after rash onset)

**Notifiable disease in Australia.** Although usually mild in children, varicella can cause severe disease in adults, immunocompromised individuals, and pregnant women.

sections.title: Clinical Presentation
sections.content:
### Prodrome (24–48 hours)
- Fever, malaise, anorexia, headache

### Rash
- Begins on face/scalp/trunk → spreads to extremities
- **Lesions in multiple simultaneous stages** (macule → papule → vesicle → pustule → crust) — distinguishes varicella from mpox/smallpox where lesions are synchronous
- Intensely pruritic
- Mucous membranes commonly involved

### Severe/Complicated Varicella
- **Varicella pneumonia:** Most common fatal complication in adults — productive cough, dyspnoea, haemoptysis, pleuritic chest pain 1–6 days after rash
- **Bacterial superinfection:** Cellulitis, abscess, necrotising fasciitis
- **CNS:** Cerebellar ataxia, encephalitis
- **Haemorrhagic varicella:** Haemorrhagic lesions, high mortality

### Herpes Zoster (Shingles) — Contact Risk
- Reactivation in dermatomal distribution — zoster is infectious to non-immune contacts via vesicle contact

sections.title: Considerations
sections.content:
### High-Risk Groups — Increased Severity
- Adults (especially >20 years): 25× higher risk of pneumonia than children
- Immunocompromised (HIV, chemotherapy, steroids)
- Pregnant women: risk of maternal pneumonia AND neonatal varicella
- Neonates (perinatal varicella: if maternal rash within 5 days pre-birth to 2 days post-birth — high neonatal mortality)
- Unvaccinated adults on mine sites (overseas-born workers may have lower immunity)

### Transmission
- **Airborne:** Highly contagious — respiratory droplets and aerosolised vesicular fluid
- **Contact:** Direct contact with lesion fluid
- Can spread in communal settings (camp dining, shared dongas) before rash appears

### Mine Site Context
- High-density accommodation substantially increases outbreak risk
- Identify potentially non-immune co-workers for public health notification
- Isolate case in private accommodation until all lesions crusted

### PPE
- P2/N95 respirator
- Gloves
- Eye protection
- Gown

sections.title: Management – Primary
sections.content:
**Apply PPE before contact**

**Uncomplicated varicella:**
- Symptomatic relief: antipyretics (avoid aspirin — Reye's syndrome risk)
- Antihistamines/calamine for pruritus
- Hygiene: keep fingernails short; advise against scratching
- Isolate from non-immune individuals

**Indications for urgent transport to hospital:**
- Dyspnoea, tachypnoea, haemoptysis (varicella pneumonia)
- Altered consciousness, seizures, ataxia (CNS involvement)
- Haemorrhagic lesions
- Pregnancy
- Immunocompromised
- Spreading cellulitis or signs of bacterial superinfection
- Neonate or infant with suspected perinatal varicella

sections.title: Management – Advanced
sections.content:
### Varicella Pneumonia
- High-flow oxygen; titrate to SpO2 ≥94%
- IV access; support with IV fluids if required
- Full PPE; nebulised medications only if clinically essential (AGP — increases airborne risk)
- Antiviral therapy (IV aciclovir) is hospital-initiated — do not delay transport

### CNS Complications
- Airway management if GCS reduced
- Manage seizures per Seizure CPG
- Urgent evacuation

### Haemorrhagic Varicella
- IV access; treat coagulopathy per haemorrhage protocol
- Urgent P1 transport

sections.title: Additional Information
sections.content:
- **Varicella is a notifiable disease** in Australia
- Varicella vaccine (VZV) is on the National Immunisation Program — two doses provide ~90% protection
- Varicella-Zoster Immunoglobulin (VZIG) available for post-exposure prophylaxis in high-risk non-immune contacts (pregnant women, neonates, immunocompromised) — must be given within 96 hours of exposure
- **Do not give aspirin** for fever — risk of Reye's syndrome in children
- Mining companies should verify staff vaccination status; non-immune workers should be vaccinated pre-deployment
